title: FINS3616 Week 7 FINAL
body:
FINS3616 INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS FINANCE

LECTURE 7 FX RISK HEDGING AND MANAGEMENT WITHIN MULTINATIONALS

Janghoon Shon (University of New South Wales)  INDIVIDUAL PROJECT

Format: Moodle submission Due: Week 11 % Evaluation: 25% The task of the project is to estimate the impact of currency on a stock and analyze the reason of the impact. Each student will submit an individual assignment online through Moodle on Friday of Week 11. The specific details of the project, as well as the specific assessment criteria, will be available on Moodle by Week 7. Students are also expected to conduct their own research for this assignment.

 2  PLAN

W1: Introduction to International finance & exchange rates W2: Description of FX markets & analysis of exchange rate risk W3: International parity conditions W4: Exchange rate systems and fundamentals W5: Exchange rate trading and forecasting W6: Flexi Week W7: Foreign currency derivatives W8: FX risk hedging and management within multinationals W9: International corporate issues W10: International financing: cross-listing and Eurobonds

 3  TODAY

1) Currency hedging with forward/futures contracts Determination of the optimal hedge ratio Discussion of hedging with futures contracts Example of imperfect hedging 2) Hedging strategy choices Duration of forward contracts Exchange rates used for hedging Estimation period 3) Example of hedging Change in exchange rates Time-varying hedge ratio

 4  TODAY

4) Tactical FX risk management and practice Discretionary hedging Institutional Investors’ hedge ratio

5) Effect of exchange rate on business valuation Multinationals with foreign sales Firms importing intermediate goods Companies in competition with multinationals

 5  TODAY

6) "Natural" exchange rate risk management in a company Choice of supply sources Choice of production site Financing abroad

7) Hedging the exchange rate risk of companies Analysis of Bodnar, Hayt, and Marston (1998) survey Wells Fargo Survey Analysis (2018) Credit Suisse study (2023)

8) Deciding whether or not to hedge exchange rate risk Reasons for and against hedging exchange rate risk

 6  HEDGING WITH FORWARD/FUTURES CONTRACTS

Hedging exchange rate risk

Long hedge • Long position in a forward contract in anticipation of a spot purchase of a foreign currency

Short hedge • Short position in a forward contract in anticipation of a spot sale of a foreign currency

 8

Intuition You work for an Australian fund investing in Japanese stocks. Next month, the Japanese stocks increase by 3% but the JPY depreciates by 2%. Your total return in AUD is then R = 3% - 2% = 1%. This is the case without hedging.

Now imagine you hedge the currency risk with forwards/futures. The return on the forward RF equals that of the spot exchange rate. You would like to short the JPY with a forward contract so that you gain when the JPY falls (to offset the loss of 2%): Your new (hedged) return is RH = R – RF = 1% – (-2%) = 3%. You have perfectly hedged currency risk in that case.

 9

Hedge ratio

Goal Determination of the exchange rate exposure to be hedged Definition Size of futures/forward position relative to the total portfolio exposure to foreign exchange rate risk Ratio h Example h = 0.6: we hedge 60% of the exchange rate movements

 10

Hedge ratio Portfolio performance with currency hedging: RH = R - h*RF

in AUD (if we are an Australian investor), with R = AUD return of the foreign portfolio without hedging h = hedge ratio (e.g., % of foreign currency exposure). [Note: negative sign to express a short hedge by default] RF = return on futures contract (AUD/X), with X=USD, JPY, ...

Q: Would a long position on the contract be good to hedge AUD depreciation or appreciation against a foreign currency X?

 11

Optimal hedge ratio (OPTIONAL)

Determination: alternative 1

Ratio hoptimal minimizes the variance σH2 of portfolio returns in AUD after exchange rate risk hedging

σH2 = σ2 + h2 σF2 - 2hcov(R, RF)

hoptimal = cov(R, RF)/σF2  dσH2 /dh = 0

where

• σ is a volatility of the portfolio return without hedging (R)

• σF is a volatility of the forward contract (RF)

• cov(R, RF) is the covariance between R and RF  12

Optimal hedge ratio (OPTIONAL)

Determination: alternative 2

Ratio hoptimal that best explains the linear relationship between (i) the return on the AUD portfolio before currency hedging and (ii) the return on the AUD/X futures contract.

• Can be estimated with an OLS regression for example.

Rt = α + hRF,t + εt

hoptimal = cov(R, RF)/σF2  dΣ(Rt-α-hRF,t )2/dh = 0

 13

Optimal hedge ratio (OPTIONAL)

Given the definition of the covariance

cov(R, RF) = correlation(R, RF) x σR x σF

We can rewrite the optimal hedge ratio as:

hoptimal = cov(R, RF)/σF2 = correlation(R, RF) x σR/σF

The optimal hedge ratio increases if

•The correlation between R and RF increases

•The volatility of R increases

•The volatility of RF declines

 14

Alternatives to improve hedging performance

• Hedge the expected value of the securities (capital gains/losses need to be accounted for!)

• Frequent hedge ratio adjustments based on portfolio performance

• Easier with futures contracts than with forward contracts

• Why? Small contract size & fixed expiry dates

 15

Some disadvantages of hedging with futures

In reality, hedging will not be perfect

• Non-constant return volatility

• Correlation between returns is not constant either

• Non-optimal contract expiry date

• Hedging only possible for short maturities

• Less than one year

• Roll-over risk

 16

Choice on the maturity of forward/futures contracts

Advantages/disadvantages of short maturity contracts

(+) More liquidity

•Lower transaction cost per contract

(+) Better replication of changes in the spot rate

•Lower basis risk

(-) Rollover to hedge for the long term

•Higher total transaction costs

 17

Choice on the exchange rates used for hedging

Practical problems

• There are no liquid forward/futures contracts for all exchange rates.

• Hedging on all currencies can be costly

Solutions

• Selection of the rates to be covered

• Some currencies are highly correlated (e.g., CHF & EUR)

• Possibility to hedge with a proxy (other currency) if the currency is not traded with enough liquidity

• Hedging against a currency index (e.g., ICE U.S. Dollar Index (USDX) futures)

 18

Estimation period for computing volatilities/correlations

Practical problem

• The future relationship between exchange rate performance and portfolio performance is not known.

Solutions

• Estimate the long-term historical relationship with statistical precision

• Problem: The relationship is unstable over time.

• Estimate the conditional relationship with shorter horizons

• Goal: generate the most recent relationship

 19

Tactical management

Practical problem

• Choice of the hedging ratio if discretionary decision

Generally used criteria

• Analyze the total risk of the portfolio and not only the risk of the portion invested in foreign countries.

• Form expectations on exchange rates

• Balance the reduction of risk and the regret of not taking advantage of favorable movements

 20

Tactical management

Institutional Investors’ hedge ratio

 21

Tactical management

In practice, the decision is often discretionary

Example: 0, 50, or 100% hedging

Many investors do not hedge against exchange rate risk.

- OK for small investors

- More serious concern for large/institutional investors

⇒ We are far from a generalized and optimal hedging.

 22

FX Management by International Fixed-Income Mutual Funds

Mutuals funds have wide variation in their exposure to foreign currencies

After hedging with FX forwards, they target an exposure around 0, 50, and 100%.

Source : Sialm, C., & Zhu, Q. (2024). Currency Management by International Fixed‐Income Mutual Funds. The Journal of Finance, 79(6), 4037-4081.  23

Introduction

Exchange rate risk is not limited to international investments.

The "domestic" portion of a portfolio should also be considered.

Examples of investment in "domestic" businesses:

• Australian multinationals with foreign sales

• Australian firms importing intermediate goods

• Australian firms competing with multinationals

 25

Multinationals with foreign sales

Effects of foreign currency depreciation

• Decrease in foreign revenues measured in domestic currency if contracts (prices) are fixed in foreign currency

=> Negative effect on investment performance

Note:

• No effect if depreciation is due to higher inflation differential (constant real exchange rate). Why?

=> What matters is the evolution of the real exchange rate.

 26

Multinationals with foreign sales

Effect of foreign currency depreciation

• If the foreign market is competitive, a company can maintain only one of the two :

• Its profitability per item sold, by increasing the selling price

• Its market share, by maintaining the selling price

But not both!

Optimal pricing policy: balance between the two

The choice will depend on

• The elasticity of foreign demand to price

• Economies of scale in production  27

Firms importing intermediate goods

Effects of foreign currency appreciation

• Increase in production costs if contracts are fixed in foreign currency

• So lower profits in domestic currency

• Think of Nike importing cotton from India.

=> Negative effect on investment performance

 28

Companies in competition with multinationals

Effects of a foreign currency appreciation

• Rising production costs abroad of direct competitors via

• Import of intermediate goods

• Outsourced production

• Increase in market share and/or profitability of companies with more domestic production

=> Positive effect on investment performance, even if the local firm has no direct exchange rate exposure

 29

Companies with financing in foreign currencies

Effects of foreign currency appreciation

• Increase in debt financing costs, when measured in domestic currency.

• Think of a Brazilian firm issuing bonds in US dollars: it costs more to finance the debt, which reduces profits and creditworthiness

=> Negative effect on bond/stock valuation for such firms

 30

Synthesis

Exchange rate fluctuations are important for the valuation of companies.

There are non-negligible effects on

• Revenues from foreign sales

• The cost of imported goods

• The nature of competition between companies, both nationally and internationally

• Interest rates on foreign currency debt

Via these channels, FX movements can affect a firm’s asset valuation and creditworthiness

 31

Illustrations

 33

1) Ability to choose your source of supply

• Abroad, when the domestic currency is strong

• Locally, when the domestic currency is weak

Supply must be available quickly

Disadvantage

A stable relationship with suppliers also has value.

• Risk of losing foreign suppliers

It is important to evaluate the costs and benefits of this flexibility.

 35

1) Ability to choose your source of supply

Alternative

• Have your own factories abroad and choose the source that minimizes costs

Disadvantage

• Expensive to establish several plants

Increase in fixed costs and decrease in economies of scale

 36

2) Produce where the product is sold

Foreign costs help offset foreign revenues

• Only profits are subject to exchange rate risk. Why?

Example

• Toyota and BMW in the 1990s

• Opening of plants in the USA to counter the depreciation of the USD

Counter-example

• Jaguar: production too low to have two profitable factories  37

3) Financing abroad

Issuance of bonds abroad (e.g., Eurobonds) with fixed interest in foreign currency

• Or use a currency swap to transform some of the liabilities in foreign currency!

Effect of foreign currency depreciation

• Decline in foreign earnings measured in domestic currency

• Decrease in interest payable on debt measured in domestic currency

• So, we can effectively offset some of the losses.

 38

Proportion of companies using derivatives as a hedge

Source : Bodnar, G. M., G. Hayt and R. C. Marston, 1998, " 1998 Survey of Financial Risk  39

Hedging by type of risk

Source : Bodnar, G. M., G. Hayt and R. C. Marston, 1998, " 1998 Survey of Financial Risk  40

Use of exchange rate derivatives

Source : Bodnar, G. M., G. Hayt and R. C. Marston, 1998, " 1998 Survey of Financial Risk  41

Hedging by maturity of foreign exchange derivatives

Ex: 44% of firms use 1-25% of derivatives with 91-180day maturity in their hedging

Source : Bodnar, G. M., G. Hayt and R. C. Marston, 1998, " 1998 Survey of Financial Risk  42

View on exchange rates and use of derivatives

Source : Bodnar, G. M., G. Hayt and R. C. Marston, 1998, " 1998 Survey of Financial Risk  43

Wells Fargo Survey Analysis (2018)

Survey Participants: 330 companies, Wells Fargo's Risk Management Clients

Main results

• Managing foreign exchange risk is objective #1

• 26% increased their foreign exchange risk management in 2018 compared to 2016

• Hedge ratio > 50%: 70%.

• Hedge < 3 months: 69%.

• Use of forward contracts: 93%.  44

Credit Suisse FX Survey (2023)

• More than 1,000 Swiss companies took part in the survey.

• Nearly 90% of these companies do business internationally, whether in the import and/or the export sector.

 45

Credit Suisse FX Survey (2023)

 46

Motivation for not hedging exchange rate risk

Decreasing profit volatility is not a sufficient reason

• Even if the firm managers are risk averse

Modigliani-Miller’s proposition indicates that a company's policy has no effect on the value of assets because

• A firm’s volatility is not remunerated/priced

• Investors can replicate all company policies

• Investors can apply an exchange rate hedge themselves.

 47

Motivation for hedging exchange rate risk

Modigliani-Miller's proposition does not hold if risk hedging:

• Affects the choice of investments

• Can be done less costly by the company than by investors

• E.g.: companies certainly have better bid-ask prices and access to long-term forward contracts.

• This reduces taxes.

• Ex: Increase in the value of the company if the tax rate is progressive (by convexity).

• Favorable FX move: high taxes (pay more).

• Unfavorable FX move: lower taxes but not 1:1 as the higher taxes.

 48

Motivation for hedging exchange rate risk

Tax

Without hedging

With hedging

Notes: Progressive marginal tax rate structure: Below 80: taxed at 10%; 80–120: taxed at 20%; Above 120: taxed at 40%  49

Motivation for hedging exchange rate risk

Some other reasons

• Offers time to adapt the business model to new conditions

• Decreases extreme movements in earnings due to exchange rates

• Lower likelihood of financial distress

• Allows to issue more debt (tax savings)

• Decreased volatility, which helps to smooth dividends over time

 50

Benefits from exchange rate volatility

Exchange rate fluctuations are not only a risk for the company

• Volatility creates investment flexibility

• Every company has « real » options that increase the value of their assets.

• « real » options = option to invest at much more favourable terms in the future

• The value of an option depends positively on volatility

 51

Benefits from exchange rate volatility

Example

Increase in exchange rate volatility

Increased likelihood of a weak foreign currency

Low-cost foreign investment opportunities

Increase in profits

Better investment performance

 52

 Adjusted Net Present Value  Adjusted Net Present Value is the sum of three things:  𝐴𝑁𝑃𝑉 = 𝑁𝑃𝑉 + 𝑁𝑃𝑉𝐹 + 𝑅𝑂  Discounted cash flows of the all-equity firm  Only incremental, after-tax cash flows (same currency!)  Value of the financial side effects  Examples: costs of issuing securities; tax implications of financing; costs of financial distress (if applicable); subsidized financing from governments  Value the real options  For a movie, this would be a sequel

 Decision: accept only (+) ANPV projects

 Example of NPVF and RO  Subsidized Financing (+)  Tax Holiday (+)  Abandonment Option (+)  Expropriation (political risk, -)  Blocked Funds (-)

 Subsidized Financing – Example  One incentive provided by the Thai government to attract Dell into setting up a local production facility is a 12year, USD30 million loan at 4%. The principal is to be repaid at the end of the 12th year. The market interest rate on such a loan is 9.50%. With a marginal tax rate of 25%, how much is this loan worth to Dell?

1 − (1 + 0.095 ∗ (1 − 0.25))−12 =𝑈𝑆𝐷30𝑚 × 9.5% − 4% × (1 − 25%) × 0.095 ∗ (1 − 0.25)

=𝑈𝑆𝐷9,763,927.08

𝑇 𝑎𝑡 1 − 𝑟 𝐺𝑖𝑣𝑒𝑛 𝑎𝑡 = 𝑎𝑡−1 ∗ 𝑟, Σ𝑡𝑇 = , 𝑓𝑜𝑟 𝑟 < 1 1−𝑟

 Tax holiday – Example  Sometimes local governments provide reduced corporate tax rates to foreign firms investing in the country.  Exemption for the first three years  Discount rate simplified

Year 0 1 2 3 4 5 EBIT (JPY) 10,000 10,000 10,000 10,000 10,000 Tax (JPY, 40%) 4,000 4,000 4,000 4,000 4,000 Tax Saving 4,000 4,000 4,000 0 0 Exchange rate (AUD/JPY) 0.01 0.01 0.01 0.01 0.01 Tax Saving (AUD) 40.00 40.00 40.00 0.00 0.00 PV (discount rate=10%) 36.36 33.06 30.05 0 0 NPV 99.47

 Country risk  Political and economic risks of operating in a country  Country recession, labor strikes, clashes  Sovereign risk  Risk associated with government defaulting on bond payments  Financial and economic risk factors  Political risk factors  Expropriation / nationalization – worst-case scenario  Taxes and regulation (i.e., hiring / firing, environmental standards, repatriation of funds)  Exchange controls (e.g., Argentina in 2002)  Corruption and legal inefficiency  Transparency International Corruption Perceptions Index for more than 170 countries

 Legal system quality varies

 The Debt Crisis (1980s)  Origins of the debt crisis  Mexico announced in 1982 they could not repay their foreign debt; by the end of the year 24 other countries followed suit

 Adjusting expected cash flows for political risk (infinite constant cash flows, constant probability of expropriation)  Compute discount rate normally (i.e., without expropriation risk)  Compute a probability distribution of expropriation risks for each future period  Compute an expected NPV based on those  Adjusting the discount rate instead of cash flows  Discount rates for emerging markets and political risk:  𝑟∗ = (𝑟+𝑝)/(1−𝑝)

 Example  Oil investment that generates an expected $50 million per year forever 50 50 50  𝑉= + +⋯= (1+𝑟) (1+𝑟)2 𝑟

 With a discount rate of 10%, the value equals $500 million.  Political risk reduces the value of this project.  The probability of an adverse event is denoted by p (constant over time) 50(1−𝑝) 50(1−𝑝)2 50(1−𝑝)  𝑉= + + ⋯ = (1+𝑟) 1 + 𝜆 + 𝜆2 + ⋯ (1+𝑟) (1+𝑟)2

1−𝑝  where 𝜆 = 1+𝑟  If 𝑆 = 1 + 𝜆 + 𝜆2 … and 𝜆 < 1, then 𝑆 = 1−𝜆

 If p = 0.12 we obtain: 50(1−𝑝) 1 50(1−𝑝) 50(1−0.12)  𝑉= × 1−𝑝 = = (0.10+0.12) = 200 (1+𝑟) 1− 𝑟+𝑝 1+𝑟

 Example  What if political risk is negligible after 1 year’s time? 50×0.88 50×0.88 44  𝑉= + +⋯= = $440𝑀 1+𝑟 (1+𝑟)2 0.10

 Notice that the probabilities in the 2nd period onward do not account for the probability of an adverse event (i.e., probability is still 88%)  You could instead simply adjust the discount rate to 11.36% (440 = 50/𝑟 ∗ )  More realistically though, there would likely be some probability of an adverse event: how about reducing it from 12% (1st year) to 1% after?  Or, adjust the discount rate to 12.50% (400 = 50/𝑟 ∗ )

50×0.88 50×0.88×0.99 50×0.88×0.992 44 43.56 0.99 0.99 2  𝑉= + 2 + +⋯= + 2 1+ + +⋯ 1+𝑟 (1+𝑟) (1+𝑟)3 1.1 1.1 1.1 1.1

 This works out to: 44 43.56 1  𝑉 = 1.1 + 1.12 = $400𝑀 1−0.9

 The PRS Group’s ICRG Rating System  Financial and economic risk factors  Assessing a country’s ability to repay foreign debt  Objective inputs

 The political risk components  Stability based on government  Subjective inputs

 Country Credit Spreads  Difference between yields of international bond and government bonds of the developed country  Sovereign credit ratings – Moody’s, S&P, Fitch  Why is sovereign credit risk different?  Cannot take a country to bankruptcy court  Still, there are consequences  Assets may be seized  Country will not be able to borrow so easily going forward  International trade could be impacted  Default could make economic crises worse

 Insurance  Coverage  Currency inconvertibility and non-transferability  Expropriation  War and political violence

 Political risk insurance for US Companies  Overseas private investment corporation (OPIC)

 Political risk insurance in emerging and transitioning economy  Multilateral investment guarantee agency (MIGA)

 Public versus private insurance  Private - more important as time goes on  Public – may deter rogue nations

 Blocked Funds  Sometimes governments hold a portion of funds and do not allow repatriation for some time or until the end of the project - capital control  What will this do to the value of the project?  The value of the project will be reduced due to the time value of money, assuming there was an opportunity cost of keeping funds abroad  Funds that are expected to be blocked in perpetuity have zero value  How would you value the project in this situation?  Value the project without the blocked funds, then value the costs of the blocked funds. Alternatively, could value the project with a new timeline of cashflows. PR = political risk  TUTOR SESSION: HEDGING AND CAPITAL BUDGETTING
